title: Chapter Thirty-Eight: The Choice
content: The security guard is new, but he knows my face.
Everyone knows my face now. That's part of the problem.
"Miss Bly." He unlatches the gate. Yellow tape flutters from the porch railing like celebration streamers at a funeral. "Fifteen minutes, yeah? I'm not supposed to—"
"Fifteen minutes."

I walk up the drive and the gravel doesn't crunch the way I remember. Maybe the rain packed it down. Maybe I'm not walking the same way I used to. The windows are boarded — ply sheets nailed crooked, gaps between them like missing teeth. Someone has spray-painted GAME OVER across the front door, but the tag is already fading. Even the vandals have moved on.
I let myself in through the side entrance. The kitchen door.

The foyer is worse than I expected. Not damaged — emptied. The photograph frames are still on the walls but the photographs are gone, taken as evidence, and what's left are rectangles of lighter wallpaper where the sun never reached. Ghost photographs. The absence of the images is more present than the images ever were. The staircase curves upward into darkness — the bulbs have been removed, or they've burned out, or the house has simply stopped bothering to illuminate itself.

The smell hits me in the dining room. Dead fireplace. Cold ash and old brick and the mineral tang of a chimney that hasn't drawn breath in weeks. I press my hand against the mantel and my palm comes away gray. I leave a print. I don't wipe it off.

Every room holds a memory. The sitting room where Helen sat with her jigsaw puzzle, piece by piece by piece, constructing a picture she could control because she couldn't control anything else. The hallway where Jessel walked on her last morning, her heels clicking on the floorboards, a sound I can still hear if I stand here long enough. The study where Peter kept his files, his monitors, his Game. The screen is gone now. The desk is gone. But the outline remains — four dark circles on the wall where the mounting brackets were, like wounds that haven't healed.
I keep walking.
Mrs. Grose is in the kitchen.

She's sitting at the table with two cups in front of her, and the kettle is still warm, and for a moment — one vertiginous moment — I think I've traveled back in time. Then I see that her hands are shaking, and the cups are mismatched, and the table has a crack running through it that wasn't there before.
"You're still here," I say.
"Where else would I be?" She doesn't look up. "Sit down. Tea's getting cold."

I sit. The chair groans under me — everything in this house groans now. The tea is Earl Grey. Real tea, not the dust-bag approximation she used to serve when the budget was tight. She must have brought it from somewhere. She must have planned this.

"I didn't want it to be empty," she says. She wraps her hands around her cup but doesn't drink. "Houses die when they're empty. You let a house sit vacant long enough, the damp gets in, and then the rot, and then the structure goes. I've seen it happen to plenty of manors in the country. Beautiful places that nobody loved enough to stay in." She finally looks at me. "I'm not leaving this one."
"They'll make you, eventually. The trust—"
"The trust can do what it likes. I'll be here when they come to board it up properly. I'll be here when they unboard it. I'll be here."

The tea is good. It occurs to me that I haven't tasted anything properly in weeks — everything since the settlement offer has been texture without flavor, input without processing. But this tea has bergamot and heat and I can feel it all the way down.
I set the cup down. "Henry offered twenty million."
"I heard."
"If we take it, the house reverts to a public trust. No trial. No testimony. But all the evidence has to be destroyed."
Mrs. Grose nods. She knew this already. Of course she knew. News travels through the village faster than it travels through the BBC.
"So," I say. "Destroy the evidence and the house goes free. Keep the evidence and the house stays on trial for years. Either way, you lose something."
"Either way," she agrees.
"What would you do?"
She sets her cup down with a click. Her eyes are red-rimmed, the skin beneath them papery and dark. She hasn't been sleeping. Maybe she hasn't been sleeping since before I arrived the first time.

"I watched a woman die," she says quietly, "because I was too tired to scream. I heard sounds in the night and I told myself it was the house settling. I told myself it wasn't my business. I told myself someone else would do something." She steadies her hands on the table. "Don't ask me what I'd do, Maeve. Ask yourself what you can live with."
I don't have an answer. She doesn't expect one. She picks up her cup and drinks, and we sit in the kitchen of Bly Manor like two people waiting for a bus that's already left.
A car engine.
I hear it through the boarded windows — a strange sound in this silence, like a radio playing in a graveyard. Mrs. Grose looks up. "That'll be Flora."
"She comes here?"
"Once a week. She likes to see the garden." A pause. "She likes to see it's still there."

I'm at the side door when Flora steps out of a blue hatchback. The car idles — someone's waiting for her. A woman in the driver's seat I don't recognize. Sarah Chen's sister, maybe. A normal person in a normal car on a normal street, living a normal life.

Flora walks toward me and I see it immediately: she looks lighter. Younger. Her hair is pulled back in a ponytail and she's wearing a jumper that's slightly too big for her, the sleeves hanging past her wrists, and she's got the beginnings of a tan — actual sun on actual skin. She's fourteen going on fifteen and for the first time since I've known her, she's acting her age.
"Maeve."
"Flora."

She stops on the step below me. Close enough to hug, but we don't. The air between us holds the scent of her — new-leaf shampoo, something green and growing, like spring in a bottle. It's so ordinary it makes my chest hurt.
"I heard about the settlement."
"Of course you did."
"What do you think?"

Her face goes still. That familiar unreadability — the mask she wore for months, the one that made her seem older than the walls around her. I think she's going to say something complicated. Something calculated. Something that sounds like a child who grew up inside Peter's Game and learned to play it before she learned to lose it.

Then her face changes. The mask doesn't crack — it dissolves. What's underneath is something I've never seen from her: simplicity. Just a girl, standing on a step, wanting something she can actually name.

"I think the truth is worth more than twenty million pounds." She says it without drama. A statement of fact, like water being wet or the sky being gray. "But I also think—" She pulls at her jumper sleeve. "I'm tired of being interesting. Can we please just be boring for a while?"

I want to say yes. I want to say: okay. I want to say: you're fourteen and you deserve boring more than anyone I've ever met. I want to say: I'll burn the files myself. I want to say: you'll never have to testify. You'll never have to sit in a courtroom and describe what Peter Quint made you watch. You'll never have to see your own words screenshotted and debated and memed by people who think your trauma is content.
I want to say yes.
I don't say anything.

Flora waits. Then she nods — she didn't expect an answer either — and walks past me into the house, and I hear Mrs. Grose's chair scrape back, and I hear the kettle start again, and I stand on the step and breathe the cold air and think about what boring costs.
My phone rings in the foyer.
I almost don't answer. The number is restricted, and I've had forty voicemails from journalists since yesterday, and I can't — I can't listen to another stranger ask me how I feel about my own life.
But I answer. Something in the timing. Something in the stillness of the house.

"Maeve." Miles's voice comes through the speaker, tinny and small, like he's calling from the bottom of a well. The facility phones are like that. They compress everything into a frequency that admits no nuance. "I heard about the deal."
"Who hasn't?"
"What do you think?"

I lean against the wall where the photographs used to hang. The lighter rectangles are right behind my head, and if I shift, I can feel their absence like a draft. "I think I'm being asked to choose between my future and my past."
"I think you should take it."
I close my eyes. "Why?"

"Because if you don't, the trial will last for years. And the internet will turn it into entertainment. You know it will. There'll be podcasts and docuseries and TikTok explainers and Reddit threads dissecting our depositions like we're characters in a fucking show. Peter's Game will become Peter's brand. They'll sell merch. They'll make action figures of the people he hurt." His voice is flat — medication or resignation or both. The facility does that to you. Smooths off the edges until everything is level, everything is manageable, everything is tolerable. "We'll never stop being content, Maeve. Take the money. Burn the files. Let the house go. And then walk away."
His voice cracks on the last word. Not the medication then. Not the resignation. Something raw underneath, something the facility hasn't managed to flatten yet.
"Please." It comes out like a splinter. "I don't want to be interesting anymore either."

The line is quiet. I can hear the buzz of the facility — fluorescent lights, distant footsteps, the mechanical hum of a building designed to contain people. Miles is contained in it. He chose it, technically. The alternative was worse. But choice is a word that stopped meaning what it's supposed to mean a long time ago in this story.
"I have to go," he says. "Visiting hours are—"
"Miles."
"Yeah?"

I want to tell him I'm sorry. I want to tell him I understand. I want to tell him that I wish I could make the world leave him alone, that I wish I could burn the files and erase the internet and make everyone forget his name. I want to promise him boring.
"I'll think about it," I say.
He hangs up.

I stand in the foyer of Bly Manor where I first entered, where the taxi dropped me on a dark evening and I walked through that door thinking I was coming to a job, thinking I was coming to a house, thinking I was coming to something I could handle.
The photograph frames are empty. The evidence taken. The walls showing their lighter rectangles like the house has been skinned.

The house is hollow. That's the word for it. Not empty — empty is when something has been removed. Hollow is when something essential was never there in the first place, or was there and was carved out, and what remains is just the shell, the shape of a thing that used to contain a purpose.
The Game is over. Peter is dead. Helen is dead. The screens are dark. And I have to choose.

Twenty million pounds. That's what the truth costs in this country, apparently. That's the price tag on the evidence — the files, the recordings, the screenshots, the documented hours of manipulation and control and damage. The settlement says: the money is yours if you pretend none of it happened. If you let the record burn. If you let the house become a garden or a museum or a wedding venue, something pretty and profitable, something that doesn't remember what it was.

The money would change my life. I could pay my mother's debts — the ones she accumulated in the years after my father left, the ones she never told me about until I found the statements in her kitchen drawer last Christmas. I could go back to Edinburgh and buy the flat on Grassmarket I've driven past a hundred times and never walked into. I could stop working for other people. I could stop walking into houses that don't belong to me and reading documents I don't want to read and discovering things I can't unknow.

The evidence would change the world. Not the whole world. Not even most of it. But it would change something. It would exist — in a courtroom, in a file, in a record that says: this happened. Here. To these people. By this design. And the next person who Googles "Bly Manor" won't just find the branding and the aesthetic and the lifestyle content. They'll find the judgment. They'll find the truth.

I think about my mother, who kept her debts in a drawer because she couldn't bear to ask for help. I think about Jessel, who walked down this hallway on her last morning and I never asked her where she was going. I think about Helen in the jigsaw-puzzle room, fitting pieces together, one by one, building a picture she could control because everything else was in someone else's hands. I think about Flora wanting to be boring. I think about Miles asking to stop being content.

I think about Peter, who would take the deal. Peter, who would laugh at the notion of principle when there was profit. Peter, who built a Game on the bet that everyone has a price, and the only question is whether you're honest enough to name it.

I am not Peter. This is the thing I have to remember. Peter used the truth as a weapon — he collected it and weaponized it and deployed it for maximum damage at maximum profit. The truth was his tool, his lever, his Game piece. He didn't care what it meant. He cared what it could do.
That's not why I'm keeping it.

I'm keeping it because the truth doesn't belong to me. It doesn't belong to Flora or Miles or Mrs. Grose or Henry or the trust or the court. It doesn't even belong to the people it hurt. It belongs to the next person. The one who hasn't arrived yet. The one who will walk into a house like Bly five years from now, or ten, or fifty, and look around and feel the hair rise on the back of her neck and think: something is wrong here. That woman deserves to find the files. She deserves to know what she's walking into. She deserves better than a pretty house with no memory and a garden where the flowers grow over the graves.

The truth doesn't exist to make people comfortable. It exists so that the next woman who walks into a house like Bly will find the record and read it and know. Not guess. Not suspect. Not feel it in her gut and tell herself she's being paranoid, like Mrs. Grose told herself the sounds weren't her business, like I told myself the Game was just a game.
Know.
I call Sarah Chen from the driveway. The security guard is watching me through the gate. The yellow tape flutters. The boards creak in the wind.
"Maeve? I've been—"
"We're not taking the deal."
Silence. Then a breath. Sarah does nothing without thinking first — it's what makes her good at her job and impossible at dinner parties. "You're sure."
"I'm sure."
"The money—"
"Is someone else's problem."
"The trial will take years. You'll testify. Flora will have to—"
"Flora won't have to do anything she doesn't choose to do. You know that. She's the one who said the truth is worth more than twenty million pounds."
A pause. "She said that?"
"She's fourteen. She's smarter than all of us."

Sarah is quiet for a long time. I can hear her pen tapping — she always taps her pen when she's thinking, a staccato rhythm like a heartbeat with arrhythmia. "I'll tell Henry's people," she says finally. "They won't be happy."
"They'll survive. They're very good at that."
I hang up. The phone is hot against my ear, the battery warm from the call, and I press it to my chest for a moment before I slide it into my pocket.

I turn and look at Bly Manor. The boards. The tape. The lighter rectangles in the windows where the photographs used to hang. I think about Mrs. Grose in the kitchen with her mismatched cups and her shaking hands. I think about Flora in her too-big jumper, smelling of new leaves. I think about Miles at the bottom of a well, asking me to let the story end.
I didn't let it end. I chose the trial. I chose the record. I chose to make them look at what they built.
The evidence stays. The story stays. The Game ends — but the truth goes on.

And so does the cost. That's the part nobody tells you about choosing what's right. The price doesn't disappear because you refused to pay it in cash. It just distributes itself differently — into years of testimony and headlines and strangers who think they know you, into Flora's boring life that won't be boring for a while yet, into Miles's hope for silence that just got louder.
I chose the truth. And now I have to live with it.

I walk to my car. I drive away. In the rearview mirror, Bly Manor shrinks to a shape, then a shadow, then a smudge against the gray June sky. Mrs. Grose is still in the kitchen. The kettle is probably on again. She'll be there when they come to unboard the windows. She'll be there when they put the photographs back, or when they leave the rectangles bare, or when the house finally learns to live with its own absence.
The truth goes on.
So does everything else.
